agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Delivery ETA communication is a core touchpoint in the courier/logistics domain. The proposal shifts from precise timestamp-based ETAs (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This fundamentally changes the delivery promise granularity and affects customer planning, operational accountability, and SLA measurement.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking & Customer Communication",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated Time of Arrival — the promised delivery window communicated to the recipient" },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The time range within which a delivery is expected to occur. Can be a precise timestamp, a time slot, or a date-only promise" },
      { "term": "SLA", "definition": "Service Level Agreement — the contractual commitment on delivery timeliness, often tied to ETA precision" },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient address, where ETA precision matters most" },
      { "term": "Delivery Promise", "definition": "The commitment shown to the customer about when their package will arrive — drives customer expectations and operational targets" },
      { "term": "On-Time Delivery Rate", "definition": "KPI measuring percentage of deliveries completed within the promised ETA window" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the recipient once a shipment enters the last-mile phase",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without an ETA, the recipient cannot plan for receipt, leading to failed deliveries and redelivery costs. An ETA of some form must always exist.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every shipment in 'out for delivery' or later status has a non-null ETA visible to the recipient"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The ETA granularity shown to the customer must match or exceed the granularity used in SLA measurement",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If SLAs are measured at hour-level precision but customers only see date-level, disputed SLA breaches become unresolvable. The customer-facing promise and internal accountability must be aligned.",
      "testable_assertion": "If the SLA is defined as 'by 14:30', the customer-facing ETA must include time-of-day precision, not just a date"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Changing ETA precision requires that all downstream systems consuming the ETA (notifications, tracking pages, partner APIs, contractual SLA reporting) are updated consistently",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Inconsistent ETA formats across channels (e.g., email says 'Friday' but tracking page says '14:30 Friday') erode trust and create support burden. System state must be consistent.",
      "testable_assertion": "The ETA format is identical across all customer-facing channels: tracking page, push notifications, email, SMS, and partner API responses"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Date-only ETAs implicitly mean 'end of business day' on the stated date unless otherwise qualified",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy choice — some companies define it as end-of-day, others as 'during business hours'. Could be changed by product decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "A date-only ETA of 'Friday' is evaluated as on-time if delivered by 23:59:59 local time on Friday"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Premium/express service tiers must retain time-precise ETAs regardless of changes to standard tier",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Premium service differentiation is a business policy, but customers paying for express service have a reasonable expectation of precise delivery windows. A VP could change this, but it would be a significant product decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Shipments with service tier 'express' or 'premium' continue to display time-of-day ETAs after the change"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A standard-tier shipment is in transit",
      "when": "The recipient views the tracking page or receives a notification",
      "then": "The ETA is displayed as a date-only value (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no time component"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A premium or express shipment is in transit",
      "when": "The recipient views the tracking page",
      "then": "The ETA retains time-of-day precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') unless explicitly scoped into the change"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The ETA format has changed to date-only",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed on any customer-facing channel (tracking page, email, SMS, push notification, partner API)",
      "then": "All channels show the identical date-only format — no channel shows a timestamp"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A shipment's date-only ETA is 'Friday'",
      "when": "The shipment is delivered at any time on Friday",
      "then": "The delivery is recorded as 'on-time' in SLA reporting"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "The system previously stored ETAs as timestamps internally",
      "when": "The display format changes to date-only",
      "then": "Internal systems continue to store and route based on full timestamps; only the customer-facing representation changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "An in-flight shipment has an existing timestamp-based ETA",
      "when": "The format change is deployed",
      "then": "The transition is handled gracefully — either the shipment retains its original format until delivery, or it is migrated to date-only with no data loss"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Shipment crosses midnight — ETA was '14:30 Friday' but now shows 'Friday', and delivery occurs at 00:15 Saturday", "expected_behavior": "Delivery is marked late. The date-only promise was Friday; Saturday delivery violates it. The wider window does not extend past the stated date." },
    { "scenario": "Customer in a different timezone than the origin warehouse", "expected_behavior": "The date-only ETA must be evaluated in the recipient's local timezone, not the sender's or warehouse's timezone" },
    { "scenario": "Same-day delivery service with date-only ETA", "expected_behavior": "Date-only is meaningless for same-day — the customer already knows 'today'. Same-day must retain time precision or at minimum a time window (AM/PM)." },
    { "scenario": "B2B customer with receiving dock hours (e.g., 08:00–16:00)", "expected_behavior": "Date-only ETA may cause failed deliveries if the courier arrives after dock hours. B2B recipients may need time-window ETAs regardless of this change." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple deliveries to same address on the same date", "expected_behavior": "Date-only ETAs become indistinguishable. The system must still allow the recipient to differentiate shipments by other attributes (tracking number, sender, contents)." },
    { "scenario": "API consumers (partners, integrations) that parse timestamp ETAs", "expected_behavior": "Removing the time component is a breaking API change. Partner integrations expecting ISO 8601 datetime will break if they receive date-only strings." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Mixed. Positive: reduced SLA breach penalties because the wider window (full day vs. specific hour) is easier to meet — on-time delivery rate will mechanically increase, potentially by 10-20%. Negative: premium customers who valued precision may downgrade or churn. B2B customers with dock-hour constraints may experience more failed first-attempt deliveries, increasing redelivery costs ($5-15 per reattempt in typical last-mile operations). Net financial impact depends heavily on customer mix (B2C vs B2B) and service tier distribution.",
    "operational": "Positive: dispatchers and route planners have more flexibility when the external promise is date-only. Reduces pressure to hit exact time slots, lowering operational stress and potentially improving route efficiency. Negative: without time-precise external accountability, internal discipline around route optimization may erode over time ('it just needs to be today'). SLA reporting systems need rework. On-time KPI becomes less meaningful as a quality signal.",
    "user": "Negative for most recipients. Precise ETAs are a top-3 feature in courier satisfaction surveys industry-wide. 'Delivery by Friday' forces the customer to be available all day rather than planning around a specific window. This is a regression in customer experience, especially for consumers who work from home or need to arrange someone to receive packages. Customers have been trained by Amazon/UPS/FedEx to expect precise windows.",
    "risk": "Medium-High. (1) Competitive risk: industry trend is toward MORE precision (2-hour, 1-hour windows), not less. Moving to date-only is counter-trend. (2) Contract risk: existing B2B contracts may specify time-precise SLAs — changing the display without reviewing contracts could create legal exposure. (3) API breaking change risk: partner integrations parsing datetime fields will break. (4) Metric gaming risk: on-time rate will artificially improve, masking real delivery performance degradation.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End recipients (B2C)", "impact": "Reduced delivery predictability. Must plan for full-day availability instead of a time window. Likely increase in 'where is my package' support contacts." },
      { "group": "End recipients (B2B)", "impact": "High risk of failed deliveries if arrival falls outside receiving hours. May require manual coordination, increasing friction and cost." },
      { "group": "Customer support team", "impact": "Likely increase in inbound contacts. Customers accustomed to precise ETAs will call/chat asking 'but WHEN today?' Volume could increase 15-25%." },
      { "group": "Operations / dispatchers", "impact": "Short-term relief from time-precise targets. Long-term risk of reduced routing discipline." },
      { "group": "Partner integrations / API consumers", "impact": "Breaking change if ETA field format changes from datetime to date. Requires migration period, versioning, or dual-format support." },
      { "group": "Sales / account management", "impact": "Harder to sell against competitors offering precise windows. B2B prospects may view date-only as inferior service." },
      { "group": "Product / analytics team", "impact": "On-time delivery KPI becomes less granular and less useful as a quality signal. Historical trend comparisons become invalid at the transition point." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The proposal is underspecified in several critical dimensions: (1) no mention of which service tiers are affected, (2) no mention of how existing SLA contracts handle the change, (3) no mention of API versioning strategy, (4) no mention of whether this applies to B2B and B2C equally, (5) the business motivation for reducing precision is unstated — is this to reduce SLA penalties, simplify the UI, or address inaccurate ETAs? The motivation fundamentally changes the correct solution. Without these answers, I cannot confidently define complete acceptance criteria.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Cross-channel consistency requirement (BR-3) is universally true",
      "B2B dock-hours edge case is a real and costly risk",
      "API breaking change risk is certain if format changes",
      "Customer experience regression is well-established by industry data"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Which service tiers are in scope",
      "Whether existing B2B contracts permit this change",
      "The actual business motivation driving this proposal",
      "Whether internal systems should also reduce precision or only the display layer",
      "How same-day delivery is handled"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Internal systems continue to calculate and store full-precision timestamps",
      "Only the customer-facing display is changing, not the underlying data model",
      "Premium/express tiers would be excluded from this change",
      "The change applies primarily to standard B2C deliveries"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The business motivation for reducing ETA precision is not stated. If the reason is that current ETAs are frequently inaccurate (e.g., showing '14:30' but delivering at 17:00), then the correct solution might be wider TIME windows ('between 12:00-18:00') rather than removing time entirely. If the reason is UI simplification, a different approach is warranted. The motivation determines the correct design.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product team", "End recipients", "Operations"],
      "options": [
        "A) Date-only for all standard shipments (as proposed)",
        "B) Wider time windows (e.g., 'Morning' / 'Afternoon' / 'Evening')",
        "C) Date-only only when confidence in time-precision is below a threshold"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — show precise ETAs when confidence is high, degrade gracefully to date-only when prediction confidence is low. This preserves value for customers while being honest about uncertainty."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of which service tiers are affected. Applying date-only ETAs to premium/express tiers would violate the value proposition those customers pay for. Need explicit scoping.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Premium customers", "Sales team", "Product team"],
      "options": [
        "A) Apply to all tiers",
        "B) Apply only to standard/economy tiers",
        "C) Apply per-tier with different granularity levels"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — standard/economy only. Premium tiers must retain time precision."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Existing B2B contracts may specify time-precise delivery SLAs. Changing the customer-facing ETA to date-only does not relieve contractual obligations. Need legal/contract review to determine if this change creates compliance exposure.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["B2B customers", "Legal team", "Account management"],
      "options": [
        "A) Exclude all B2B accounts from the change",
        "B) Review contracts first, then scope accordingly",
        "C) Change display but maintain contractual time-precise commitments internally"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — contracts must be reviewed before any change to B2B-facing ETAs."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The API impact is unaddressed. If the ETA field in partner-facing APIs changes from datetime to date, this is a breaking change requiring versioning. If only the UI changes but the API retains timestamps, partners are unaffected but there's a consistency gap.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Partner integrations", "Engineering team", "API consumers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Change API field to date-only (breaking change with migration period)",
        "B) Keep API as datetime, change only UI display",
        "C) Add new date-only field alongside existing datetime field"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — additive API change avoids breaking partners while supporting the new display format."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
